SE1.tagline: Design and build scalable cloud applications across compute, storage, and managed services
SE1.subtitle: cloud architecture decisions, scalable application patterns, managed compute, storage trade-offs, and production delivery
foundations: Cloud compute placement: virtual machines, containers, Kubernetes, serverless functions, and managed application platforms
Cloud storage semantics: object, block, file, relational, document, key-value, cache, queue, and stream systems
CAP theorem and the availability, consistency, partition-tolerance trade-off
Replication, partitioning, indexing, caching, and eventual-consistency models for cloud data
Stateless service design, idempotency, backpressure, retries, timeouts, and circuit breakers
Reconciliation and the declarative desired-state control loop
Queueing theory and tail-latency analysis under load
Autoscaling control theory and capacity versus cost models
Supply-chain provenance, reproducible-build theory, and deployment safety

literature.title: A View of Cloud Computing
literature.authors: Michael Armbrust, Armando Fox, Rean Griffith, Anthony D. Joseph, Randy Katz, Andy Konwinski, Gunho Lee, David Patterson, Ariel Rabkin, Ion Stoica, Matei Zaharia

literature.title: CAP Twelve Years Later: How the Rules Have Changed
literature.authors: Eric Brewer

literature.title: Borg, Omega, and Kubernetes
literature.authors: Brendan Burns, Brian Grant, David Oppenheimer, Eric Brewer, John Wilkes

literature.title: Large-scale Cluster Management at Google with Borg
literature.authors: Abhishek Verma, Luis Pedrosa, Madhukar Korupolu, David Oppenheimer, Eric Tune, John Wilkes

literature.title: Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store
literature.authors: Giuseppe DeCandia, Deniz Hastorun, Madan Jampani, Gunavardhan Kakulapati, Avinash Lakshman, Alex Pilchin, Swaminathan Sivasubramanian, Peter Vosshall, Werner Vogels

literature.title: The Google File System
literature.authors: Sanjay Ghemawat, Howard Gobioff, Shun-Tak Leung

SE1.build: Designed and engineered a scalable cloud application with an API, background workers, object storage, relational or NoSQL persistence, queues or events, caching, CDN delivery, and containerized or serverless compute, provisioned through Terraform, load-tested with k6, and instrumented with Prometheus and Grafana against latency, reliability, and cost goals.
objectives: Compare cloud compute options: virtual machines, containers, Kubernetes, serverless functions, managed application platforms, and managed databases.
Choose appropriate storage for workload requirements: object storage, block storage, file storage, relational databases, document stores, key-value stores, caches, queues, and streams.
Design a scalable cloud application around stateless services, durable state, asynchronous workflows, caching, and content delivery.
Apply cloud architecture patterns including API gateway, worker queue, event-driven integration, fan-out, materialized views, cache-aside, bulkhead, retry, circuit breaker, and idempotent consumer.
Containerize application components and decide when Kubernetes is appropriate versus serverless or managed platform deployment.
Model availability, consistency, latency, throughput, and cost trade-offs in cloud architecture decisions.
Provision infrastructure declaratively using infrastructure as code and environment-specific configuration.
Configure load balancing, autoscaling, and deployment strategies that meet defined latency and availability targets.
Instrument services with metrics, logs, traces, alerts, and dashboards tied to service-level objectives.
Defend architecture choices using design diagrams, load-test evidence, failure-mode analysis, and cost estimates.
prerequisites: Basic Linux command line and networking
Familiarity with HTTP and web services

weeks.title: Cloud application architecture and provider landscape
weeks.lecture: Survey cloud service models, regions, availability zones, managed services, shared responsibility, and the design difference between lifting a server and building for cloud elasticity.
weeks.practice: Compare the same small application deployed as a virtual machine, container service, and managed application service, then map the operational responsibilities of each option.
weeks.project: Project repository, cloud account, workload scenario, initial architecture diagram, and baseline hello-service are established.

links.title: Cloud Native Concepts: Docker and Kubernetes Fundamentals (freeCodeCamp)
CAP Theorem Explained

weeks.title: Compute choices: VMs, containers, Kubernetes, serverless, and managed platforms
weeks.lecture: Compare virtual machines, containers, Kubernetes, serverless functions, batch jobs, and managed runtimes by startup time, isolation, scaling model, state constraints, cost, and operational burden.
weeks.practice: Containerize one service, deploy a simple serverless function, and document which compute model fits each component of the project.
weeks.project: Project compute decision matrix is delivered and the core API runs in a container with a clean, layered, reproducible image.

links.title: What Containers Are: Docker and Kubernetes course section (freeCodeCamp)
Whats Under the Hood: Namespaces, Cgroups and OverlayFS

weeks.title: Storage choices: object, block, file, relational, NoSQL, cache, queue, and stream
weeks.lecture: Compare cloud storage services by access pattern, consistency, durability, query model, latency, scale, backup model, and cost.
weeks.practice: Implement one project feature twice using different storage choices, then justify the final storage model for structured data, blobs, cache, and asynchronous messages.
weeks.project: Project data model, storage decision record, object-storage integration, and first durable persistence layer are implemented.

links.title: Docker Images and CLI: Docker and Kubernetes course section (freeCodeCamp)

weeks.title: Designing scalable cloud application structure
weeks.lecture: Design stateless services, externalized state, configuration, sessions, health checks, rate limits, request correlation, and twelve-factor application boundaries.
weeks.practice: Refactor the project into an API service, worker boundary, persistence layer, and externalized configuration with health and readiness checks.
weeks.project: Project exposes a stateless API service with externalized state and documented service boundaries.

links.title: Microservices Explained: the What, Why and How (TechWorld with Nana)

weeks.title: Architecture specification milestone
weeks.lecture: Review workload requirements, architecture decision records, threat boundaries, scaling goals, storage choices, compute choices, and cost assumptions.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: user flows, data model, compute and storage choices, scaling goals, reliability targets, and cost budget.
weeks.project: Approved architecture specification, decision records, and project implementation plan are delivered.

weeks.title: Networking, API gateways, load balancing, and content delivery
weeks.lecture: Analyze VPCs, subnets, security groups, DNS, TLS, API gateways, load balancers, ingress, CDN caching, and edge delivery.
weeks.practice: Expose the project through an API gateway or ingress, add TLS and routing rules, and serve static or media assets through object storage and a CDN.
weeks.project: Project is reachable through a managed entry point with documented network boundaries, TLS, routing, and CDN or object-storage delivery.

weeks.title: Asynchronous workflows, queues, events, and background workers
weeks.lecture: Design worker queues, publish-subscribe events, fan-out, scheduled jobs, idempotent consumers, dead-letter queues, and backpressure.
weeks.practice: Move a slow project workflow behind a queue or event bus, add a background worker, and make processing idempotent and observable.
weeks.project: Project includes an asynchronous workflow with durable messaging, worker processing, retry policy, and failure handling.

links.title: What is Helm in Kubernetes? Helm and Helm Charts Explained (TechWorld with Nana)

weeks.title: Scalable design patterns milestone
weeks.lecture: Cover microservice decomposition, bounded contexts, cache-aside, materialized views, outbox, saga, bulkhead, retry, timeout, circuit breaker, and graceful degradation.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo showing a scalable workflow, a storage decision, an asynchronous path, and one resilience pattern under failure.
weeks.project: Working cloud application is demonstrated with architecture diagrams, pattern choices, and measured behavior under a realistic load path.

links.title: Microservices Explained: the What, Why and How (TechWorld with Nana)

weeks.title: Container platforms and Kubernetes when they are the right fit
weeks.lecture: Study Kubernetes control plane concepts, deployments, services, ingress, config, secrets, persistent volumes, Helm packaging, and when Kubernetes complexity is justified.
weeks.practice: Deploy the project to Kubernetes or a managed container platform with manifests or Helm and compare it against a simpler managed-service option.
weeks.project: Project has a documented container-platform deployment path and a justified Kubernetes versus managed-platform decision.

links.title: Istio Service Mesh Tutorial: Ingress Gateway, Virtual Service, mTLS

weeks.title: Serverless and managed-service architectures
weeks.lecture: Design with functions, managed runtimes, managed databases, object storage triggers, event buses, API gateways, scheduled jobs, and cold-start and limit-aware constraints.
weeks.practice: Implement one project component as a serverless or managed-service flow and compare latency, cost, failure handling, and operational effort against the containerized version.
weeks.project: Project includes a serverless or managed-service component with clear integration boundaries and measured trade-offs.

links.title: Terraform Explained in 15 mins: Tutorial for Beginners (TechWorld with Nana)

weeks.title: Infrastructure as code, environments, and deployment safety
weeks.lecture: Discuss declarative provisioning, state management, modules, secrets, environment promotion, blue-green and canary releases, rollback, and configuration drift.
weeks.practice: Provision the project environment from Terraform, separate dev and production-like variables, and run a controlled deployment with rollback.
weeks.project: Project infrastructure and deployment pipeline are reproducible from versioned code with documented environment configuration.

links.title: Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) Explained
Basics of Load Testing with k6 and Grafana in 20 Minutes (k6)

weeks.title: Scaling, reliability, observability, and cost optimization
weeks.lecture: Analyze horizontal and vertical scaling, load balancing, autoscaling signals, queue depth, cache hit ratio, latency percentiles, SLOs, and cost models for elastic versus reserved capacity.
weeks.practice: Run load tests, tune autoscaling or concurrency, add dashboards and alerts, and produce a cost analysis with optimization actions.
weeks.project: Project meets defined latency and throughput targets under load, with observability, alerts, and a cost optimization report in place.

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize cloud architecture principles and review the path from requirements to compute, storage, networking, reliability, scalability, and cost decisions.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of architecture, compute choices, storage choices, scaling evidence, failure handling, and cost decisions.
weeks.project: Final scalable cloud application is delivered with architecture documentation, load-test evidence, operational dashboards, and defense.

project.summary: Teams design and build one scalable cloud application from workload requirements through architecture decision records, then implement an API, durable storage, object storage, asynchronous workers, caching or CDN delivery, and a deployment path using containers, serverless functions, or managed platforms. The system is provisioned through infrastructure as code, load-tested, instrumented, and defended with evidence for scalability, reliability, and cost.

tools: Docker: build and run application containers
Kubernetes: orchestrate containerized workloads
Helm: package and template Kubernetes manifests
Istio: manage service to service traffic and policy
Terraform: provision cloud infrastructure as code
AWS Lambda: implement serverless functions
Amazon S3: provide object storage for files, logs, and static assets
Amazon RDS: provide managed relational database storage
Amazon DynamoDB: provide managed key-value and document storage
Amazon SQS and Amazon SNS: provide durable queues and pub-sub messaging
Amazon API Gateway: expose managed HTTP APIs
Amazon CloudFront: deliver content through a CDN
Redis: provide low-latency caching and rate-limit state
PostgreSQL: model relational cloud application data
Prometheus: collect cluster and application metrics
Grafana: visualize metrics and build dashboards
Amazon Web Services: host managed cloud services
kubectl: operate clusters from the command line
Trivy: scan container images for vulnerabilities
k6: run load and performance tests

SE1.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to compare cloud architecture options, draft architecture decision records, scaffold Terraform modules, generate Dockerfiles, sketch API and worker boundaries, and produce candidate storage schemas for relational, document, key-value, cache, queue, and object-storage services. They use chat-based and editor-integrated tools to generate k6 load scripts and realistic traffic, then connect agents to kubectl and cloud MCP servers to inspect deployment state and propose fixes. AI also helps interpret Prometheus and Grafana output, summarizing latency, queue depth, cache hit ratio, and cost data into concrete autoscaling and design actions. Teams are expected to review every generated architecture and manifest critically, since a plausible but wrong compute or storage choice can create a system that cannot scale.

SE2.tagline: Engineer software that is secure, tested, and reliable by design
SE2.subtitle: secure-by-design engineering, systematic testing methodology, QA process artifacts, automation frameworks, and reliability under formal objectives
foundations: Saltzer and Schroeder protection principles and least privilege
STRIDE threat modeling over data-flow trust boundaries
Black-box test design: equivalence partitioning, boundary-value analysis, decision tables, pairwise testing, and state-transition testing
White-box and fault-based test design: coverage criteria, mutation testing, and risk-based testing theory
Test strategy, test plans, traceability matrices, defect lifecycle, quality metrics, test reports, and release-readiness evidence
Test pyramid design, component tests, API contract testing, and end-to-end workflow validation

Software quality metrics: defect density, escaped defects, defect leakage, coverage, mutation score, flaky-test rate, cyclomatic complexity, duplication, maintainability, vulnerability aging, and SLO attainment
Static data-flow and taint analysis with soundness trade-offs
Trusting-trust and supply-chain integrity reasoning
Service level objectives, error budgets, and reliability theory
High-cardinality telemetry and the three pillars of observability

literature.title: The Protection of Information in Computer Systems
literature.authors: Jerome H. Saltzer, Michael D. Schroeder

literature.title: Reflections on Trusting Trust
literature.authors: Ken Thompson

literature.title: Hints on Test Data Selection: Help for the Practicing Programmer
literature.authors: Richard A. DeMillo, Richard J. Lipton, Frederick G. Sayward

literature.title: Why Cryptosystems Fail
literature.authors: Ross Anderson

literature.title: OWASP Top 10:2021
literature.authors: OWASP Foundation

literature.title: Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) Version 1.1, NIST SP 800-218
literature.authors: Murugiah Souppaya, Karen Scarfone, Donna Dodson

SE2.build: Hardened a web application end to end: threat-modeled it with OWASP Threat Dragon, built a QA strategy with a test plan, risk matrix, traceability matrix, test cases, defect reports, quality-metrics dashboard, and release-readiness report, automated unit, integration, REST API, contract, schema, component, end-to-end UI, cross-browser, accessibility, mutation, and performance tests, remediated OWASP Top 10 risks, and built a GitHub Actions CI pipeline gating on tests, coverage, mutation score, complexity, duplicated code, Semgrep SAST, OWASP ZAP DAST, dependency scanning, accessibility checks, and k6 smoke performance thresholds, with OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, and Grafana enforcing service level objectives.
objectives: Explain the principles of secure software development across the lifecycle
Analyze the OWASP Top 10 risks and apply secure coding countermeasures
Construct threat models using data-flow diagrams and trust boundaries
Evaluate dependency and supply-chain risks and apply provenance controls
Apply SAST and DAST tooling and interpret their findings critically
Design test cases using equivalence partitioning, boundary-value analysis, decision tables, pairwise combinations, state-transition models, and exploratory charters
Design automated test suites grounded in coverage, risk, acceptance criteria, and fault-based theory
Produce professional QA artifacts: test strategy, test plan, requirements traceability matrix, test-case repository, defect reports, test summary report, and release recommendation
Define, collect, interpret, and defend software quality metrics for product quality, process quality, test effectiveness, security posture, maintainability, and operational reliability
Automate unit, integration, component, REST API, schema, contract, browser end-to-end, cross-browser, accessibility, mutation, and smoke performance tests
Build continuous integration and delivery pipelines with quality gates
Report, triage, reproduce, and verify defects using professional QA workflows
Conduct effective code reviews focused on correctness and security
Instrument systems for observability with metrics, logs, and traces
Reason about reliability using service level objectives and error budgets

weeks.title: Secure Development and Threats
weeks.lecture: Introduce security principles, the CIA triad, attacker models, and the secure development lifecycle.
weeks.practice: Audit the starter project, enumerate assets, and set up the repository and toolchain.
weeks.project: Project repository, threat scope, and security baseline are established.

links.title: Hands-On Cybersecurity and Ethical Hacking Full Course (freeCodeCamp)

weeks.title: OWASP Top 10 and Secure Coding
weeks.lecture: Examine injection, broken access control, and cryptographic failures with their root causes.
weeks.practice: Find and fix seeded OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities in the project code.
weeks.project: Project passes a first pass of OWASP Top 10 remediation.

links.title: An Introduction to the OWASP Top 10
2021 OWASP Top Ten Overview
SQL Injection Attacks For Beginners

weeks.title: Threat Modeling
weeks.lecture: Teach STRIDE, data-flow diagrams, trust boundaries, and risk prioritization theory.
weeks.practice: Produce a threat model of the project with prioritized mitigations.
weeks.project: A documented threat model with a mitigation backlog is delivered.

links.title: STRIDE Threat Modeling for Beginners in 20 Minutes

weeks.title: Software Testing Foundations
weeks.lecture: Cover test oracles, coverage criteria, equivalence partitioning, boundary-value analysis, decision tables, state-transition testing, pairwise testing, risk-based testing, the test pyramid, mutation testing, and fault-based testing theory.
weeks.practice: Derive test cases from requirements using equivalence classes, boundaries, decision tables, and state models, then automate unit, integration, REST API, and browser end-to-end tests with request logs, response assertions, screenshots, traces, and reproducible defect evidence.
weeks.project: Project gains a baseline automated test suite with measured coverage, REST API regression tests, core user-flow E2E tests, browser trace evidence, a test-case repository, a traceability matrix, and a defect register.

links.title: Equivalence Partitioning in Software Testing
Testing JavaScript with Cypress Full Course (freeCodeCamp)

weeks.title: Specification Milestone
weeks.lecture: Review test strategy, test plans, security requirements, acceptance criteria, traceability, quality metrics, defect severity, test-environment planning, entry and exit criteria, and how quality attributes shape design.
weeks.practice: Student teams present their project specification: security requirements, threat model, risk-based test matrix, acceptance tests, automation plan, test data plan, defect workflow, quality-metrics plan, and reliability plan.
weeks.project: Approved specification with security goals, measurable quality goals, traceability matrix, QA process artifacts, quality-metrics plan, and a test-automation roadmap is delivered.

links.title: Eliciting and Specifying Quality Attribute Requirements (SEI Carnegie Mellon)

weeks.title: Continuous Integration and Delivery
weeks.lecture: Discuss pipeline theory, deployment automation, test fixtures and mocks, test doubles, flaky-test control, test reporting, coverage gates, mutation-score gates, complexity thresholds, artifact retention, and the economics of fast feedback.
weeks.practice: Build a CI pipeline that runs unit, integration, component, REST API, OpenAPI schema, contract, browser E2E, cross-browser, and accessibility checks, publishes JUnit and Allure-style reports, records coverage and mutation score, runs static quality analysis, and blocks merges on failures.
weeks.project: Project has a CI pipeline enforcing automated QA gates, browser regression checks, coverage thresholds, mutation thresholds, static quality thresholds, and retained test evidence on every change.

links.title: CI/CD Explained in 7 Minutes
Jenkins CI/CD Pipeline Full Course (freeCodeCamp)

weeks.title: SAST and Static Analysis
weeks.lecture: Explain static analysis theory, data-flow and taint tracking, and false-positive trade-offs.
weeks.practice: Integrate a SAST scanner into the pipeline and triage its findings.
weeks.project: Pipeline runs static security analysis as a gated stage.

links.title: Semgrep 101: Static Analysis and SAST

weeks.title: Interim Demo Milestone
weeks.lecture: Cover dynamic analysis, fuzzing concepts, exploratory testing, session-based test management, defect triage, root-cause analysis, defect metrics, escaped-defect analysis, and how they complement static and automated testing.
weeks.practice: Student teams present an interim demo of the secured project, its pipeline, current findings, exploratory charters and notes, defect lifecycle, quality dashboard, and test-summary evidence.
weeks.project: Secured project with working CI, analysis, exploratory QA evidence, defect triage, a quality-metrics dashboard, and a test summary report is demonstrated.

links.title: Fuzzing Programs to Find Bugs (Computerphile)

weeks.title: DAST and Dependency Security
weeks.lecture: Examine dynamic scanning, software composition analysis, and supply-chain provenance models.
weeks.practice: Run DAST against the project and add dependency and SBOM scanning.
weeks.project: Project enforces dynamic and dependency security checks.

links.title: Learn OWASP ZAP in 8 Minutes

weeks.title: Code Review and Secure Authentication
weeks.lecture: Discuss review effectiveness research, authentication, session management, and access control design.
weeks.practice: Conduct structured peer code reviews and harden authentication in the project.
weeks.project: Project has reviewed code and hardened authentication and access control.

links.title: Code Review Best Practices, Trisha Gee
A High Level Overview of Server Side Sessions for Authentication

weeks.title: Reliability and SRE
weeks.lecture: Introduce service level objectives, error budgets, reliability engineering principles, load testing, operational quality metrics, and failure-mode validation.
weeks.practice: Define SLOs for the project, run a k6 smoke load test, add resilience to a critical path, and connect reliability metrics to release criteria.
weeks.project: Project defines SLOs, records performance thresholds, tracks operational quality metrics, and improves a critical-path failure mode.

links.title: SRE Fundamentals: SLIs, SLOs, SLAs and Error Budgets

weeks.title: Observability
weeks.lecture: Cover the three pillars of observability and the theory of high-cardinality telemetry.
weeks.practice: Instrument the project with metrics, logs, traces, quality dashboards, and alerting.
weeks.project: Project emits structured telemetry with dashboards, alerts, and a release-readiness view combining test, defect, security, maintainability, and reliability metrics.

links.title: OpenTelemetry Fundamentals: Traces, Metrics and Logs Explained

weeks.title: Final Demo and Defense
weeks.lecture: Synthesize secure, tested, and reliable engineering and review the full quality pipeline.
weeks.practice: Student teams present the final demo with an oral defense of security, QA strategy, traceability, test evidence, quality metrics, defect decisions, automation gates, and reliability decisions.
weeks.project: Final secure and reliable system is delivered with test plan, traceability matrix, automated test reports, coverage and mutation evidence, quality-metrics dashboard, defect history, release recommendation, documentation, and defense.

project.summary: Teams take one web application and engineer it to be secure, tested, and reliable end to end. They turn requirements into acceptance criteria, maintain a risk-based QA plan, traceability matrix, test-case repository, exploratory charters, defect register, test summary reports, quality-metrics dashboard, and release-readiness evidence. They automate unit, integration, component, REST API, OpenAPI schema, contract, browser end-to-end, cross-browser, accessibility, mutation, and smoke performance tests, threat-model the app, remediate OWASP Top 10 risks, build a CI and CD pipeline with SAST, DAST, dependency scanning, coverage gates, mutation gates, static quality gates, and QA gates, and add observability and service level objectives. Application security and product quality are revisited every week as new attack surfaces and regression risks appear.

tools: JUnit or pytest: write unit and integration tests
Mockito or unittest.mock: isolate units with test doubles and mocks
JaCoCo or coverage.py: measure statement, branch, and line coverage
Stryker or mutmut: run mutation testing and expose weak assertions
SonarQube: track code smells, complexity, duplication, maintainability, reliability, and security hotspots
Allure Report: publish readable automated test evidence
Playwright, Cypress, Selenium, or WebdriverIO: automate end-to-end browser workflows with screenshots, videos, traces, and selectors
BrowserStack: run cross-browser and cross-device regression checks
Robot Framework: express keyword-driven acceptance and regression tests
Vitest or React Testing Library: automate front-end component tests
Postman, Bruno, Insomnia, or Hoppscotch: explore REST APIs, save collections, define environments, and document manual checks
Newman: run Postman REST API collections in CI
REST Assured, Karate, or Supertest: automate REST API regression, auth, negative, and data-driven tests in code
OpenAPI: document REST contracts and generate validation targets
Schemathesis or Dredd: validate and fuzz REST APIs against OpenAPI schemas
Pact: verify consumer-driven API contracts between services
axe DevTools: audit accessibility compliance
k6: run smoke performance and load tests
Jira or TestRail: manage test cases, defects, and verification status
GitHub Actions: run continuous integration pipelines
OWASP ZAP: perform dynamic application security testing
Semgrep: run static application security testing
Snyk: scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities
OWASP Dependency-Check: detect vulnerable libraries
Trivy: scan images and generate software bill of materials
OWASP Threat Dragon: build and document threat models
OpenTelemetry: instrument services for traces and metrics
Prometheus: collect reliability metrics and define alerts
Grafana: visualize service level objectives and telemetry

SE2.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate and refactor application code, then turn the same tools on themselves to find and remediate OWASP Top 10 weaknesses through guided code review. They prompt AI to expand unit, integration, REST API, schema, contract, and browser E2E suites, propose equivalence classes and edge cases, draft exploratory charters, sharpen defect reports, and generate GitHub Actions pipeline steps that wire in Postman/Newman, REST Assured, Karate, Supertest, Schemathesis, Dredd, Playwright, Cypress, Selenium, WebdriverIO, accessibility checks, k6 smoke tests, Semgrep, OWASP ZAP, and dependency scans. Agents connected to repository, browser, API, and scanner MCP servers help triage test failures, browser traces, API request/response logs, SAST findings, and DAST findings, separating real issues from false positives. AI further helps analyze telemetry and error budgets, but every security fix, test, and defect disposition is verified by the team, since vibe-coded changes can quietly introduce new attack surface or false confidence.

project.summary: Teams design and build one single-page web application that grows from a static shell into a component-based, state-managed product. The application is made responsive, accessible to WCAG standards, and tuned to performance budgets with an optimized build. Every increment is validated through component and interaction tests.

tools: React: build component-based user interfaces
Vue: build reactive component interfaces
TypeScript: add static typing to front-end code
Vite: bundle and serve the application during development
Redux Toolkit or Pinia: manage application state
React Router or Vue Router: handle client-side routing
Tailwind CSS: compose responsive utility-based styles
Vitest: run unit and component tests
Playwright: run end-to-end interaction tests
axe DevTools: audit accessibility compliance
Lighthouse: measure performance and best practices
SE3.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to scaffold React or Vue components, refactor prop and event contracts, and convert rough sketches into typed, composable interfaces. They prompt tools to generate Vitest and Playwright tests, mock API data, and propose accessible ARIA markup, then ask the AI to audit components against WCAG and performance budgets. Editor-integrated agents and browser or Lighthouse MCP connections help diagnose render bottlenecks and bundle bloat and suggest code-splitting changes. Because generated UI code can silently break keyboard navigation or accessibility, students validate every suggestion against real assistive-technology and performance checks.

The relational model and normalization theory
ACID transactions, isolation levels, and indexing internals
Token and federated identity flows for authentication
Cache coherence, invalidation, and consistency trade-offs
Replication, statelessness, and the CAP trade-off
Role and attribute-based access control and least privilege

SE4.build: Built a back-end API platform on Node.js or FastAPI exposing both REST and an Apollo GraphQL layer over a normalized, indexed PostgreSQL schema, secured with JWT authentication and role-based access control, accelerated with Redis caching, and load-tested with k6 against scaling targets.
objectives: Explain the architectural constraints of REST and resource-oriented design
Compare REST and GraphQL and reason about their trade-offs
Model relational and non-relational data and analyze normalization
Design and implement APIs with versioning and clear contracts
Implement authentication, sessions, and authorization securely
Apply caching strategies and reason about consistency and invalidation
Analyze database indexing, transactions, and query performance
Scale back-end services horizontally and handle concurrency
Validate APIs with automated and contract testing
Evaluate consistency, availability, and latency trade-offs in data systems
prerequisites: Familiarity with HTTP and web fundamentals

project.summary: Teams build one back-end service that grows from basic endpoints into a complete API platform with a tuned data layer. The service exposes both REST and GraphQL, enforces secure authentication and authorization, and adds caching and scaling to meet load targets. Each increment is validated with automated and contract tests.

SE4.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate REST and GraphQL handlers, refactor resolvers, and draft normalized SQL schemas with sensible indexes and migrations. They prompt tools to write contract and integration tests, seed realistic data, and build k6 load scripts, while connecting agents to database and API MCP servers to inspect query plans and propose tuning. AI helps reason about caching and invalidation strategies and draft JWT and role-based access rules, which students then probe for authorization gaps. Every generated query and auth rule is reviewed, since a plausible-looking AI suggestion can leak data or trigger an N+1 performance cliff.

Synchronization protocols and conflict detection

Build cross-platform mobile applications with a modern framework
Analyze mobile UI, lifecycle, and resource-constrained design
Apply IoT messaging protocols such as MQTT and CoAP
Integrate sensors and actuators with constrained devices
Design edge computing architectures and reason about latency trade-offs
Implement offline-first applications with local storage
Apply data synchronization and conflict resolution strategies
Evaluate security and privacy concerns across device boundaries
Assess trade-offs between cloud, edge, and device computation

SE6.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to scaffold Flutter or React Native screens, refactor widget trees and lifecycle code, and generate device-storage and sensor integration. They prompt tools to write MQTT and CoAP client code, simulate sensor streams, and produce sync and conflict-resolution logic, while connecting agents to broker and device MCP endpoints to inspect message flows. AI helps reason about offline-first state, eventual consistency, and CRDT merge behavior, and to generate tests for intermittent connectivity. Because generated sync code can corrupt data under partition or conflict, students test every suggestion against realistic offline and edge scenarios.

SE7.tagline: Architect data-intensive and AI-driven systems at scale
SE7.subtitle: data systems theory, distributed consensus, ETL and ELT pipelines, Spark data processing, and the operational debt of machine learning
foundations: Reliability, scalability, and maintainability as system properties
Storage-engine internals and data-model trade-offs
ETL and ELT pipeline design from raw ingestion to curated analytical datasets
The MapReduce model of large-scale batch computation
Apache Spark execution, PySpark transformations, and lakehouse processing with Databricks
Event-time dataflow, watermarks, and windowing theory
Replication, partitioning, and the CAP and PACELC trade-offs
Distributed consensus and exactly-once processing semantics
Hidden technical debt and operational concerns in ML systems

SE7.build: Architected a data-intensive system that ingests events through Apache Kafka, runs ETL and ELT flows with PySpark on Apache Spark and Databricks, processes streams with Flink into a replicated, partitioned Cassandra store, serves predictions from an MLflow-tracked model, and guards quality with Evidently drift monitoring.
objectives: Explain the foundations of reliability, scalability, and maintainability
Analyze data models, storage engines, and indexing trade-offs
Design batch and streaming data pipelines
Implement ETL and ELT transformations with PySpark, Apache Spark, and lakehouse tables
Reason about distributed storage, replication, and partitioning
Apply the theory of consistency, consensus, and the CAP trade-off
Integrate machine learning models into production systems
Evaluate architectural trade-offs for AI-based and data-intensive workloads
Address technical debt and operational concerns in ML systems
Design for fault tolerance and exactly-once processing semantics
Assess data quality, lineage, and governance in pipelines

project.summary: Teams build one data-intensive system that ingests data, processes it through ETL and ELT flows with PySpark, Apache Spark, and optionally Databricks, and stores it in a replicated, partitioned distributed store. They integrate a machine learning model for serving, add data-quality and drift monitoring, and reason explicitly about consistency, fault tolerance, and architectural trade-offs throughout.

tools: Apache Kafka: stream and buffer event data
Apache Spark: run batch ETL and distributed processing
PySpark: implement Spark transformations and DataFrame pipelines in Python
Databricks: develop notebooks, jobs, and lakehouse workflows on managed Spark
Apache Parquet: store columnar analytical datasets
Apache Flink: process streams with event-time semantics
PostgreSQL: store relational and derived data
Apache Cassandra: provide distributed partitioned storage
Apache Airflow: orchestrate data pipelines
dbt: transform and model warehouse data
Great Expectations: validate ETL outputs and data contracts
MLflow: track and serve machine learning models
Feast: manage a feature store
Docker: package pipeline and serving components
Evidently: monitor data and model drift

references.note: Managed Spark, notebooks, jobs, and lakehouse workflows

SE7.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants to generate PySpark DataFrame transformations, Spark SQL queries, Databricks notebooks and jobs, Flink transformations, and Kafka producers, consumers, and schema definitions. They prompt tools to build ETL data-quality checks, synthesize event streams, and write tests for exactly-once and fault-tolerance paths, while connecting agents to database, Databricks, MLflow, and pipeline MCP servers to inspect state and metrics. AI helps wire model serving, feature processing, and Evidently drift monitoring, and to analyze lineage and operational telemetry into remediation steps. Because generated distributed code can hide consistency, shuffle, partitioning, or replay bugs, students validate every pipeline change against partitioning, replication, and delivery-guarantee behavior.
